title: Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Emergency Medicine, Psychiatry, Critical Care
objectives: Identify causative medications including dopamine antagonists
Differentiate from serotonin syndrome and malignant hyperthermia
Manage with supportive care, bromocriptine, and dantrolene

vignette.text:
A 28-year-old man with schizophrenia is brought from a group home with fever, confusion, and muscle stiffness. Haloperidol was started 10 days ago. Staff noted progressive lethargy and difficulty walking over several days.

Past Medical History: Schizophrenia

Medications: Haloperidol 5mg BID (started 10 days ago)

Allergies: NKDA

physicalExam.neuro: Confused, severe diffuse rigidity (lead-pipe), hyporeflexia, tremor

questions.stem: This patient has fever, rigidity, altered mental status, and elevated CK after starting haloperidol.
questions.question: What is the clinical tetrad of NMS?

options.text: NMS CLINICAL TETRAD: (1) HYPERTHERMIA: Often extreme (>40°C/104°F); From muscle hyperactivity; (2) LEAD-PIPE RIGIDITY: Severe, generalized; NOT cogwheel (that's Parkinsonism); (3) ALTERED MENTAL STATUS: Confusion → obtundation → coma; Fluctuating consciousness; (4) AUTONOMIC INSTABILITY: Tachycardia, labile BP; Diaphoresis; Sialorrhea; Urinary incontinence; ADDITIONAL FEATURES: Hyporeflexia (key differentiator from SS); Decreased bowel sounds; Tremor; Mutism; Dysphagia; LABS: CK markedly elevated (often >1000, can be >100,000); Leukocytosis; Elevated LFTs; Myoglobinuria; Metabolic acidosis

questions.explanation: NMS Clinical Tetrad: HYPERTHERMIA (often >104°F), LEAD-PIPE RIGIDITY (severe, generalized), ALTERED MENTAL STATUS (confusion to coma), AUTONOMIC INSTABILITY (tachycardia, labile BP, diaphoresis). Additional: Hyporeflexia (key vs SS), decreased bowels, tremor. LABS: Markedly elevated CK (>1000, can be >100,000), leukocytosis, elevated LFTs, myoglobinuria.

sources.text: StatPearls: Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: He was started on haloperidol 10 days ago.
questions.question: What medications cause NMS?

options.text: NMS CAUSATIVE AGENTS: DOPAMINE ANTAGONISTS: Typical antipsychotics: Haloperidol (highest risk), Chlorpromazine, Fluphenazine; Atypical antipsychotics: Risperidone, Olanzapine, Quetiapine, Clozapine, Aripiprazole; Antiemetics: Metoclopramide, Prochlorperazine, Promethazine; DOPAMINE WITHDRAWAL: Sudden L-dopa discontinuation in Parkinson's; RISK FACTORS: High-potency antipsychotics (haloperidol); IM administration; Rapid dose escalation; Dehydration; Agitation/exhaustion; Prior NMS episode (17-30% recurrence); Organic brain disease; ONSET: Typically within 2 weeks of starting (90%); Can occur at any time; Average onset 4-14 days

questions.explanation: NMS Causative Agents: DOPAMINE ANTAGONISTS including typical antipsychotics (haloperidol highest risk), atypical antipsychotics (all can cause), antiemetics (metoclopramide, prochlorperazine). Also from DOPAMINE WITHDRAWAL (sudden L-dopa stop). RISK FACTORS: High-potency agents, IM route, rapid escalation, dehydration, prior NMS. ONSET typically within 2 weeks (90%), average 4-14 days.

sources.text: StatPearls: Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: The diagnosis of NMS is confirmed.
questions.question: How is NMS managed?

options.text: NMS MANAGEMENT: STOP OFFENDING AGENT: Critical first step; SUPPORTIVE CARE: ICU admission; Aggressive cooling (target <38.5°C); IV fluids (prevent AKI from rhabdomyolysis); Cardiac monitoring; Intubation if needed; SPECIFIC THERAPIES: BROMOCRIPTINE (dopamine agonist): 2.5mg PO/NG q8h, titrate to 45mg/day; Restores dopamine; May take days to work; DANTROLENE (muscle relaxant): 1-2.5 mg/kg IV, repeat q6h; Max 10 mg/kg/day; For severe rigidity/hyperthermia; Both can be used together; BENZODIAZEPINES: For agitation; Adjunct for rigidity; ECT: For refractory cases; AVOID: Restarting causative agent; Physical restraints; Antipyretics (ineffective); DURATION: Oral agents: 7-14 days (days to weeks resolution); Depot: May last weeks

questions.explanation: NMS Management: STOP OFFENDING AGENT immediately. SUPPORTIVE: ICU, aggressive cooling, IV fluids (rhabdomyolysis prevention), monitoring, intubation PRN. SPECIFIC: BROMOCRIPTINE 2.5mg q8h (dopamine agonist), DANTROLENE 1-2.5 mg/kg IV q6h (muscle relaxant) - can use both together. Benzodiazepines for agitation. ECT for refractory. Continue treatment 7-14 days; resolution takes days to weeks.

sources.text: StatPearls: Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Strawn JR, et al. Neuroleptic malignant syndrome. Am J Psychiatry. 2007;164(6):870-876. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: What are the complications of NMS?

options.text: NMS COMPLICATIONS: RHABDOMYOLYSIS: Elevated CK from muscle rigidity; Can cause AKI; ACUTE KIDNEY INJURY: From myoglobinuria; From dehydration; May require dialysis; RESPIRATORY FAILURE: Chest wall rigidity; Aspiration; ARDS; DIC: From tissue destruction; VTE: Immobility; CARDIOVASCULAR: Arrhythmias; MI; Cardiac arrest; INFECTIONS: Aspiration pneumonia; UTI; Sepsis; MORTALITY: 5-20% with treatment; Higher without recognition; RISK OF RECURRENCE: 17-30% if rechallenged; Wait ≥2 weeks after resolution; Use low-potency agent; Start low, titrate slowly; Monitor closely

questions.explanation: NMS Complications: RHABDOMYOLYSIS from rigidity causing AKI from myoglobinuria. Respiratory failure from chest rigidity/aspiration. DIC, VTE, arrhythmias, infections. MORTALITY 5-20% with treatment. RECURRENCE RISK 17-30% if rechallenged - wait ≥2 weeks, use low-potency agent, start low and titrate slowly.

sources.text: StatPearls: Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Strawn JR, et al. Neuroleptic malignant syndrome. Am J Psychiatry. 2007;164(6):870-876. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.question: What is the complete management approach?

options.text: Complete approach: (1) RECOGNIZE NMS TETRAD: Hyperthermia (104°F) + Lead-pipe rigidity + AMS + Autonomic instability; (2) CONFIRM: CK 18,500 (markedly elevated), hyporeflexia, onset after haloperidol = NMS; (3) STOP HALOPERIDOL immediately; (4) ICU ADMISSION; (5) AGGRESSIVE SUPPORTIVE CARE: Cooling to <38.5°C; IV fluids (prevent AKI from rhabdomyolysis); Cardiac monitoring; Intubation if needed; (6) SPECIFIC TREATMENT: Bromocriptine 2.5mg q8h; Dantrolene 1-2.5 mg/kg IV if severe rigidity; Benzodiazepines for agitation; (7) MONITOR: Serial CK (rhabdomyolysis); Creatinine (AKI); Urine output; Respiratory status; (8) DURATION: Continue treatment 7-14 days; (9) FUTURE: If antipsychotics needed, wait ≥2 weeks; Use different agent at low dose

questions.explanation: Complete NMS Approach: RECOGNIZE tetrad (hyperthermia, rigidity, AMS, autonomic instability). CONFIRM with markedly elevated CK, hyporeflexia. STOP HALOPERIDOL. ICU with aggressive cooling, fluids (rhabdo prevention), monitoring. SPECIFIC: Bromocriptine + Dantrolene if severe. Monitor CK, creatinine, urine output. Continue treatment 7-14 days. FUTURE antipsychotics: Wait ≥2 weeks, use different low-potency agent.

sources.text: StatPearls: Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Strawn JR, et al. Neuroleptic malignant syndrome. Am J Psychiatry. 2007;164(6):870-876. Accessed January 14, 2026.
